mappings.rationaleAI: Australian history from colonial foundations through the Mabo decision on Aboriginal land rights, the White Australia policy, and federation directly addresses Australia since 1920 (B), including interwar developments and the Great Depression (B.1), postwar political and economic developments including Aboriginal programs (B.2), international relations (B.3), and social developments including Aboriginal-rights issues and immigration (B.4).
British imperial administration and decolonization connect to the post-World War I mandate system in Oceania (A.1) and Australia's relationship with Britain in the interwar period (B.1).

Transition from colonial empires to independent states connects to post-World War II movements toward autonomy among indigenous peoples and independent island states (A.3) and independence of Papua New Guinea (B.3).

Japan's expansion in the Pacific and the rise and fall of Japanese power address World War II in the Pacific and effects on indigenous peoples (A.2) and Australia's military role in World War II (B.1).
Cold War superpower rivalry and proxy wars connect to Australia's participation in regional security alliances and military involvement in the Korean and Vietnam wars (B.3).
The UN trusteeship system administered Pacific territories and guided the dissolution of the Trust Territory of the Pacific Islands (A.3).
International migration and immigration policy connect to demographic and cultural changes resulting from large-scale European and Asian immigration to Australia (B.4).
Welfare state systems connect to the expansion of social services under the Whitlam government (B.2) and New Zealand Labour Party social welfare programs (C.1).
Democracy and democratic governance connect to Australia's parliamentary system and the 1975 constitutional crisis and dismissal of Whitlam (B.2).

mappings.vsiTitle: The Great Depression and The New Deal
mappings.rationaleAI: The Great Depression's global impact connects to the Great Depression in Australia and formation of the United Australia Party (B.1) and New Zealand's Great Depression and Labour Party victory (C.1).
Feminism and women's movements connect to feminism and the women's movement as social developments in Australia from 1945 (B.4).
Nuclear weapons policy connects to Australia's opposition to French nuclear testing in the South Pacific (B.3).
Human rights frameworks connect to Aboriginal land-claims legislation (B.2) and Aboriginal-rights issues (B.4).
Ethnographic study of non-Western societies connects to indigenous peoples of Oceania and their movements toward autonomy (A.3).
Globalization and economic integration connect to Australia's strengthening of economic and political ties to Asian and Pacific nations (B.3).
Nationalism and self-determination connect to movements toward autonomy among the indigenous peoples of Oceania and independent island states (A.3).
Japan's militarism and World War II involvement connect to the rise and fall of Japanese power in the Pacific and effects of the war on indigenous peoples (A.2).
